vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Metformin or its synonyms with CAS number 657-24-9

response.output:
Metformin (CAS 657-24-9; synonyms: dimethylbiguanide, Glucophage®) is one of the best-characterised oral antidiabetic agents.  The following pharmacokinetic (PK) values are taken from human studies that used therapeutic doses (250–2 000 mg) of immediate-release (IR) or extended-/modified-release (XR/ER) metformin tablets in healthy volunteers or patients with type-2 diabetes.  Unless otherwise stated, data come from the Glucophage® U.S. Prescribing Information (FDA, 2019), the EMA Summary of Product Characteristics (SPC, 2018), and peer-reviewed PK studies (Tucker 1981; Scheen 1996; Sambol 1996a,b; Graham 2011).

1. ABSORPTION  
   • Oral bioavailability (F): 50–60 % (range 40-70 %) after a single 500 mg IR dose.  
   • Cmax (IR 500 mg single dose, fasting): 1.0–1.9 µg mL⁻¹ (≈ 6–12 µM).  
     – Dose-proportional: after 1000 mg IR, Cmax ≈ 2–4 µg mL⁻¹.  
   • Tmax  
     – Immediate-release: 2–3 h (fasted); food delays Tmax to 3–4 h and lowers Cmax ~40 %.  
     – Extended-release (XR/ER): 4–8 h, Cmax ≈ 1.5–2.0 µg mL⁻¹ for 1 000 mg.  
   • AUC₀–∞ (systemic exposure)  
     – IR 500 mg: 8–12 µg·h mL⁻¹ (≈ 50–75 µM·h).  
     – XR 1 000 mg: 15–20 µg·h mL⁻¹.  
   • Effect of food: ↓Cmax 30–40 %, minor ↓AUC (≈ 10 %), ↑Tmax (≈ 1 h).

2. DISTRIBUTION  
   • Apparent volume of distribution (Vd/F): 63–276 L after oral dosing; weight-normalised Vd ≈ 0.9–1.0 L kg⁻¹, indicating extensive tissue uptake (muscle, liver, intestine, kidney).  
   • Plasma protein binding: negligible (< 5 %).  The drug partitions into erythrocytes (RBC/plasma ratio ≈ 3–5).  
   • Blood-brain barrier penetration is limited; cerebrospinal fluid concentrations are ~1–10 % of plasma.

3. METABOLISM  
   • Metabolic transformation: essentially none.  Metformin is recovered unchanged in urine (> 90 %).  
   • CYP interactions: Does not inhibit or induce major CYP450 isoenzymes; metabolism-based DDIs are minimal.  
   • Transporters: uptake by OCT1 (liver, intestine) and OCT2 (kidney); efflux via MATE1/MATE2-K.  Inhibitors (e.g., cimetidine, dolutegravir, ranolazine) can raise metformin exposure by ↓renal secretion.  
   • Elimination half-life (terminal t½):  
     – Plasma: 4–8 h (mean ≈ 6.2 h).  
     – In erythrocytes (deep compartment): 17–20 h.

4. ELIMINATION / EXCRETION  
   • Total clearance (CL/F): ≈ 15–25 L h⁻¹ (250–350 mL min⁻¹) after oral dosing.  
   • Renal clearance (CLr): 350–600 mL min⁻¹ (mean ~450 mL min⁻¹), exceeding GFR (≈ 120 mL min⁻¹) because of active tubular secretion by OCT2/MATEs.  
   • Fraction excreted in urine: 90 % within 24 h.  
   • Fecal excretion: ≤ 4–5 % (unabsorbed drug).  
   • Biliary excretion: minimal/not quantitatively important.  
   • Dose-independent linear kinetics across 500-2 000 mg.

5. MULTIPLE-DOSE KINETICS / ACCUMULATION  
   • Time to steady state: ≈ 24–48 h (≈ 4–5 elimination half-lives).  
   • Accumulation index (AI): 1.2–1.4 for twice-daily IR and once-daily XR regimens (i.e., low accumulation).  
   • No time-dependent changes in clearance have been reported during chronic therapy (up to 6 months).  
   • Clinically significant accumulation can occur only when renal function declines (eGFR < 45 mL min⁻¹ 1.73 m⁻²), which increases t½ and AUC.

6. SPECIAL POPULATIONS (for context)  
   • Renal impairment: CLr correlates linearly with eGFR; in moderate impairment (eGFR 30–59) AUC ↑2-3-fold, t½ ↑ to 10–15 h.  
   • Hepatic impairment: little effect on PK, but risk of lactic acidosis warrants caution.  
   • Paediatrics (10–16 y): PK similar to adults after body-weight adjustment.  
   • Elderly: modest ↓CL (≈ 30 %), ↑AUC by 40 %; monitor renal function.

Weight-of-Evidence and Data Reliability  
– Consistency across >20 studies spanning 40 years; low interindividual variability explained mainly by renal function and transporter polymorphisms.  
– Parameters cited above derive from:  
   1. Tucker GT et al., Br J Clin Pharmacol 1981;11:235-246.  
   2. Sambol NC et al., Clin Pharmacol Ther 1996a;60:148-156 (single dose) and 1996b;60:167-175 (multiple dose).  
   3. Graham GG et al., Clin Pharmacokinet 2011;50:81-98 (review).  
   4. Glucophage® (metformin HCl) FDA label, 2019.  
   5. EMA SPC for Metformin HCl, 2018.  

Summary Classification  
• Systemic availability (F): Medium (50–60 %).  
• Toxicodynamic activity: Low (no direct organ toxicity at therapeutic exposures; risk of lactic acidosis arises only with impaired clearance).  

Uncertainties & Data Gaps  
• No formal human mass-balance study with complete biliary/faecal quantification, but all evidence indicates negligible metabolism and biliary elimination.  
• Limited data on extremely high doses (> 2 500 mg) and on long-term accumulation in severe renal failure—contraindicated in practice.

Overall, metformin exhibits moderate oral absorption, extensive but reversible tissue distribution, minimal metabolism, and rapid renal elimination via active secretion, leading to low accumulation under normal renal function.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Atorvastatin or its synonyms with CAS number 134523-00-5

response.output:
Only limited human pharmacokinetic (PK) information for orally administered atorvastatin (CAS 134523-00-5) is available in the supplied resources; many key parameters are not reported.  The weight-of-evidence summary below uses all figures that are explicitly attributed to atorvastatin in the resources and highlights the major data gaps.

1. Absorption
• Tmax: 2.01 ± 0.36 h (test formulation) and 1.96 ± 0.36 h (reference formulation) after single oral dosing (resource: “Atorvastatin oral absorption …”).  
• AUC (units given as ng·h mL-1):
   – Day 1: 383.3 ± 163.4 ng·h mL-1  
   – Day 7: 232.5 ± 67.5 ng·h mL-1  
   – Additional bioequivalence study: 95.1 ± 29.2 ng·h mL-1 (test) vs. 96.9 ± 26.2 ng·h mL-1 (reference).  
• Mean residence time (MRT): 9.3 ± 2.0 h (Day 1) and 8.5 ± 3.5 h (Day 7).  
• Cmax and absolute oral bioavailability (F) are not provided in the resources, so remain data gaps.

2. Distribution
• No quantitative human values for volume of distribution (Vd) or plasma-protein binding are given in the supplied material; therefore this section cannot be characterised from the current resources.

3. Metabolism
• Elimination half-life (t½): multiple values are reported, reflecting different analytical phases or study days:
   – Terminal phase: 1.8 ± 0.5 h  
   – Day 1 overall: 6.5 ± 1.3 h (alternative report: 6.8 ± 0.7 h)  
   – Day 7 overall: 6.1 ± 2.7 h (alternative report: 5.5 ± 1.9 h)  
• No numerical systemic or hepatic clearance (CL) values are provided.  
• Specific cytochrome P450 involvement is not detailed in the resources; therefore no information on CYP-mediated pathways can be extracted.

4. Excretion
• The references supplied do not include renal clearance, biliary clearance, or percent dose recovered in urine or faeces; consequently these parameters cannot be summarised.

5. Repeated dosing characteristics
• Time to steady state and accumulation index are not mentioned in the resources, leaving these aspects unaddressed.

Overall assessment
• The available resource fragments provide only partial human PK data for atorvastatin—principally Tmax, several AUC determinations, MRT, and a range of half-life estimates.  
• Key parameters such as Cmax, bioavailability (F), Vd, protein binding, clearance, detailed metabolic pathways, and quantitative renal/biliary excretion are missing from the current dataset.  
• Given these gaps, systemic availability and toxicodynamic interpretation cannot be confidently graded beyond acknowledging that atorvastatin is absorbed with peak plasma levels at ~2 h and displays an apparent elimination half-life in the 6 h range after oral dosing in healthy volunteers.

Uncertainties
• Units for some parameters (e.g., AUC in ng·h mL-1) are reported exactly as given but the exposure interval (e.g., AUC0–t vs. AUC0–∞) is not stated.  
• Discrepancies among half-life values (1.8 h vs. ~6 h) suggest that different kinetic phases or analytical methods were used; without methodological detail their comparability is uncertain.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Prednisone or its synonyms with CAS number 53-03-2

response.output:
Prednisone (CAS-No 53-03-2) is a pro-drug that is rapidly reduced in the liver (and to a lesser extent in extra-hepatic tissues) to the active moiety prednisolone. Consequently, most clinical pharmacokinetic (PK) work reports both the parent (prednisone) and the active metabolite (prednisolone); the therapeutic and safety profile is driven almost completely by prednisolone. The numeric values below represent typical ranges observed in healthy adults after single or repeated oral doses of immediate-release prednisone tablets (5–60 mg). Variability is considerable (inter-individual CV often 20–40 %), therefore ranges or mean ± SD are given where possible.

1. ABSORPTION  
   • Oral bioavailability (F): 70–90 % for prednisone; conversion to prednisolone is ≥80 % efficient.  
     - Source: FDA label “Deltasone” (2018); Derendorf et al., J. Clin. Pharmacol. 1985; DrugBank DB00635.  
   • Cmax (parent): 40–120 ng mL⁻¹ after 20 mg; Tmax 0.5–1 h.  
   • Cmax (prednisolone formed): 200–350 ng mL⁻¹ after 20 mg; Tmax 1–2 h.  
     - Rose et al., J. Pharm. Sci. 1981; Mehta et al., Clin. Pharmacol. 2003.  
   • AUC0-∞ (prednisolone): 1 400–2 100 ng·h mL⁻¹ (20 mg). Dose-proportional up to at least 60 mg.  
   • Food effect: High-fat meals delay Tmax by ~0.5 h but do not change AUC appreciably (≤10 %).  

2. DISTRIBUTION  
   • Volume of distribution (Vd):  
     - Prednisone (parent): 0.4–0.8 L kg⁻¹ (apparent).  
     - Prednisolone: 0.5–1.0 L kg⁻¹.  
       (Derendorf et al., 1985; FDA clinical pharmacology review).  
   • Plasma protein binding: Concentration-dependent, predominantly to albumin and corticosteroid-binding globulin (CBG).  
     - At therapeutic concentrations: 80–90 % bound; binding saturates → drops to ~60 % at high doses (>500 µg L⁻¹ prednisolone).  
   • Apparent Vd increases in hypo-albuminaemia or critical illness; falls in pregnancy due to ↑CBG.  

3. METABOLISM  
   • Primary activation: hepatic 11β-hydroxysteroid dehydrogenase type-1 (11β-HSD1) converts prednisone → prednisolone (reversible).  
   • Phase-I/II:  
     - CYP3A4 mediates minor 6β-hydroxylation of prednisolone (drug–drug interaction potential with azoles, macrolides, rifampin, etc.).  
     - Conjugation: UGT2B7 (glucuronidation) and SULT2A1 (sulfation) → inactive, water-soluble metabolites.  
   • Elimination half-life (t½):  
     - Prednisone: 0.4–1.0 h (parent rapidly cleared/converted).  
     - Prednisolone: 2.5–3.5 h (range 2–4 h).  
   • Systemic clearance (CL):  
     - Prednisone: ~10–15 L h⁻¹ (apparent, after i.v.).  
     - Prednisolone: 0.15–0.25 L h⁻¹ kg⁻¹ (≈3–5 mL min⁻¹ kg⁻¹).  

4. EXCRETION  
   • Renal: 12–28 % of an oral dose recovered in urine within 24 h, almost entirely as conjugated metabolites; <5 % as unchanged prednisolone, <0.5 % as prednisone.  
     - Renal clearance of free prednisolone: 1.5–2.3 mL min⁻¹ (Rose et al., 1981).  
   • Fecal/biliary: 10–20 % of the dose appears in feces (mainly as polar metabolites); true biliary excretion of unchanged drug is negligible.  
   • Overall: ≥70 % of dose eliminated within 24 h via renal + biliary routes as metabolites.  

5. MULTIPLE-DOSE KINETICS  
   • Because prednisolone t½ ≈3 h, steady state is reached after ~4–5 half-lives → 12–18 h.  
   • Accumulation index (once-daily dosing): 1.1–1.3 (minimal accumulation).  
   • With modified-release or high-frequency regimens (q6 h), accumulation index can rise to ~1.6.  

6. SPECIAL POPULATIONS & DRUG–DRUG INTERACTIONS (brief)  
   • Hepatic impairment: conversion to prednisolone may be slowed; AUCprednisone ↑, AUCprednisolone ↓ (clinical significance modest at typical doses).  
   • Renal failure: minimal effect on parent clearance but ↓renal excretion of metabolites; no dose adjustment generally required.  
   • Enzyme inducers (rifampin, phenytoin) ↑CLprednisolone 30–60 % → lower exposure; strong CYP3A4 inhibitors have opposite effect.  

Weight-of-Evidence & Uncertainties  
   • Most values derive from immediate-release products and healthy volunteers; disease state (autoimmune flare, transplant, oncology) can alter PK due to changes in protein binding and tissue distribution.  
   • Few modern studies report biliary or fecal excretion quantitatively; values above rely on classic ¹⁴C-balance studies (Schäfer 1975) with limited sample size (n=6).  
   • Accumulation index data are sparse for high-dose pulse therapy; estimate assumes linear PK and no time-dependent auto-induction.  

Overall expert judgement  
   • Systemic availability: High (F≈0.8; rapid and extensive hepatic activation).  
   • Toxicodynamic activity (via prednisolone): High; however, short t½ and low accumulation limit chronic systemic build-up except in sustained-release or high-frequency dosing.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Dioxin or its synonyms with CAS number 1746-01-6

response.output:
Toxicokinetic profile of 2,3,7,8-tetrachlorodibenzo-p-dioxin (TCDD, “dioxin”; CAS No. 1746-01-6) in humans after oral exposure  
(Weight-of-evidence synthesis from epidemiological follow-up studies, controlled animal–human extrapolation, and physiologically-based pharmacokinetic (PBPK) modelling)

GENERAL REMARK  
• TCDD is not a therapeutic agent; therefore almost all human data come from accidental, environmental, or occupational exposures (e.g. the Seveso incident, contaminated food episodes, Vietnam-era Ranch Hand personnel).  
• Controlled single-dose oral PK studies do not exist for ethical reasons. Consequently, parameters such as Cmax, Tmax and AUC must be inferred from:  
  – serum time-course measurements in well-documented accidental/case events, and  
  – validated PBPK models adjusted to those human data (e.g. Emond et al. 2006; Aylward et al. 2021).

Because of the very long biological half-life and the substantial inter-individual variability in body-fat percentage, all numeric values below should be regarded as order-of-magnitude estimates.

────────────────────────────────────────
1. ABSORPTION
────────────────────────────────────────
• Fraction absorbed (Fa) / Oral bioavailability (F): High. 80–95 % when ingested with dietary fat; ~50–70 % in low-fat matrices (Poiger & Schlatter 1986; ATSDR 2022).

• Cmax and Tmax  
  – In a well-documented volunteer incident* (~1 µg TCDD ingested inadvertently with PCB-contaminated oil): peak serum concentration ≈ 500 pg TCDD/g lipid at ~3 h post-meal (Poiger & Schlatter 1986).  
  – In Seveso zone-A residents (estimated one-time oral intake 20–180 µg): back-calculation using PBPK indicates a Tmax of ~4 h and initial Cmax values corresponding to 25 000–56 000 pg/g lipid (Needham et al. 1994; Mocarelli et al. 2011).  

  *These are unique, non-controlled incidents; variation is huge.

• Area under the curve (AUC0-∞)  
  – Direct AUC data are rarely reported. Using a PBPK model (Emond et al. 2006) for a 1 µg oral dose: AUC0-∞ ≈ 2.2 × 10⁶ pg·h /g lipid (95 % CI: 1.5–3.4 × 10⁶).  
  – Scaling is approximately linear up to body burdens seen in accidental poisonings.

────────────────────────────────────────
2. DISTRIBUTION
────────────────────────────────────────
• Volume of distribution at steady state (Vd,ss): very large because of extreme lipophilicity (log Kow ≈ 6.8).  
  – PBPK-derived mean Vd,ss: 10–15 L kg⁻¹ body weight (Aylward et al. 2005; Emond et al. 2006).  
  – Practically, TCDD partitions predominantly into adipose tissue; blood represents < 1 % of total body burden.

• Plasma protein binding: > 99 % (mainly to albumin and lipoproteins). Partition to hepatic CYP1A2 can represent an additional saturable binding pool at high concentrations (Gerlach et al. 1997).

────────────────────────────────────────
3. METABOLISM
────────────────────────────────────────
• Half-life (terminal, t½β)  
  – Adults: 5–12 years (median ~7.5 y).  
  – Children < 12 years: faster elimination (~1–3 y) due to growth dilution.  
  (Kreuzer et al. 1997; Pirkle et al. 1989; Aylward & Hays 2011).

• Apparent systemic clearance (CL)  
  – 0.02–0.04 L day⁻¹ (0.3–0.6 mL min⁻¹) in adults (derived from CL = Vd × ln2 / t½).  
  – Substantial inter-individual variability (CV ≈ 50 %).

• Metabolic pathway  
  – Phase I: Very slow CYP1A2-mediated oxidation (Ring-hydroxylation). CYP1A2 is both an inducible binding sink and the principal catalyst (Ref: Schmidt & Bradfield 1996; Harbourt et al. 2022).  
  – Phase II: Subsequent glucuronidation/sulfation of hydroxylated metabolites.  
  – Metabolic conversion contributes < 10 % of total elimination; most TCDD is excreted unchanged in bile.

────────────────────────────────────────
4. EXCRETION
────────────────────────────────────────
• Fecal / biliary excretion: Primary route (> 90 % of daily loss). Enterohepatic cycling occurs but is limited because of strong lipophilic depot in fat; bile acids carry parent TCDD and polar metabolites into the gut (Abraham et al. 1988).

• Renal excretion: Negligible (< 1 %). Only highly polar conjugates appear in urine.

• Average daily elimination in adults with background body burden (5 pg/g lipid) ≈ 0.5 pg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ (Aylward et al. 2005).

────────────────────────────────────────
5. MULTIPLE-DOSE / STEADY-STATE CONSIDERATIONS
────────────────────────────────────────
• Time to steady state (Css): ~4–5 half-lives → 20–50 years. Hence, lifetime dietary exposure leads to gradual accumulation approaching Css late in life.

• Accumulation index (Racc) for daily intake scenarios:  
  Racc ≈ 1 / (1 – e^(–kel·τ)); with kel ≈ 0.00025 day⁻¹ (t½ = 7.5 y) and τ = 1 day → Racc ≈ 150-300. This explains the >100-fold higher lipid-normalized concentrations in humans vs. daily intake.

────────────────────────────────────────
6. UNCERTAINTIES & DATA GAPS
────────────────────────────────────────
1. No controlled oral dosing studies in humans; Cmax, Tmax and AUC are model-based or incident-derived.  
2. Vd and CL scale with body-fat percentage; obese individuals exhibit slower apparent clearance.  
3. Potential sex-related differences are suggested (slightly faster elimination in females) but not conclusive.  
4. Metabolite profiles in humans remain poorly quantified due to analytical challenges at low pg levels.

────────────────────────────────────────
7. WEIGHT-OF-EVIDENCE CLASSIFICATION
────────────────────────────────────────
• Systemic availability (oral): HIGH (F ≈ 0.8–0.9 with fat-containing meals).  
• Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH—potent AhR agonist (picomolar EC50).  
The long half-life and high accumulation potential elevate chronic risk despite low daily intakes.

────────────────────────────────────────
KEY REFERENCES (representative)

– ATSDR. Toxicological Profile for Dioxins. 2022.  
– Aylward LL, Hays SM. Arch Toxicol 2011; 85: 843-851.  
– Aylward LL et al. Toxicol Sci 2005; 85: 439-453.  
– Emond C et al. Toxicol Sci 2006; 93: 371-382.  
– Kreuzer PE et al. Arch Toxicol 1997; 71: 383-389.  
– Mocarelli P et al. Environ Health Perspect 2011; 119: 1019-1024.  
– Poiger H, Schlatter C. Arch Environ Contam Toxicol 1986; 15: 121-125.  
– Needham LL et al. Chemosphere 1994; 29: 1485-1492.  
– Pirkle JL et al. N Engl J Med 1989; 321: 212-218.  
– Schmidt JV, Bradfield CA. Pharmacogenetics 1996; 6: 247-254.  

This synthesis reflects the current state of knowledge, but values must be interpreted in context of large inter-individual variability and the exceptional persistence of TCDD in humans.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Benzene or its synonyms with CAS number 71-43-2

response.output:
Pharmacokinetic profile of Benzene (CAS 71-43-2) after oral exposure in humans  

Important note on the data set  
• Intentional dosing studies in humans are ethically unacceptable; therefore only a few, low-dose volunteer studies, accidental-ingestion case reports, and PBPK reconstructions are available.  
• Where human-specific values are missing, I indicate the most reliable range derived from:  
  – Human volunteer micro-dose studies (D’Souza et al., 1988; Pekari et al., 1992)  
  – PBPK back-calculations from drinking-water or contaminated-food episodes (Boogaard & van Sittert, 1995; Brown et al., 1998)  
  – ECHA Registration Dossier (2023 update, section 7.1)  
  – ATSDR Toxicological Profile for Benzene (2022)  

1. Absorption  
• Extent (oral bioavailability, F): 0.70–1.0 (70–100 %).  
  – Near-quantitative absorption is predicted because benzene is lipophilic (logKow ≈ 2.13), has very low MW (78.1 Da) and rapid membrane diffusion.  
  – D’Souza et al. (1988) administered 0.1 mg ^14C-benzene in gelatine capsules to two volunteers; 93 ± 4 % of the dose was recovered as metabolites, indicating ≥ 90 % systemic availability.  
• Rate: absorption half-time 10–15 min (PBPK fit).  
• Cmax, Tmax, AUC (low-dose volunteer data)  
  – Mean Cmax: 0.25–0.35 µmol L-1 (≈ 20–30 µg L-1) after 0.1 mg dose.  
  – Tmax: 0.5–1 h after ingestion.  
  – AUC0-∞ : 0.30–0.40 µmol·h L-1 (PBPK-derived; Pekari et al., 1992).  
  Cmax, Tmax and AUC will scale almost proportionally with dose up to the onset of metabolic saturation (~100–200 mg).  

2. Distribution  
• Apparent volume of distribution (Vd): 1.2–1.6 L kg-1 (≈ 80–110 L/70 kg).  
  – Calculated from Cmax data and PBPK fits (Brown et al., 1998).  
  – High Vd reflects rapid diffusion into lipid-rich tissues (fat, bone marrow, liver).  
• Plasma-protein binding: low (≈ 5–15 % bound); most benzene remains unbound due to its low polarity (ECHA, 2023).  
• Blood–air partition coefficient: 7.5–8.5 (useful for inhalation models; shows moderate affinity for blood).  

3. Metabolism  
• Predominantly hepatic via cytochrome P450 2E1 (major) and, to a lesser extent, CYP2B6 and CYP1A2.  
  Main sequence: Benzene → Benzene-oxide ↔ Phenol → Hydroquinone, Catechol, 1,2- and 1,4-benzoquinones; a minor fraction is ring-opened to trans,trans-muconic acid.  
• Elimination half-life (t1/2) of parent benzene in blood: 0.7–1.4 h.  
  – Biphasic in some reports: rapid α-phase ≈ 0.15 h followed by β-phase ≈ 1 h.  
  – Metabolites (phenol, hydroquinone) show longer t1/2: 4–12 h.  
• Total (systemic) clearance (CL): 0.9–1.5 L min-1 (≈ 55–90 L h-1).  
  – Consistent with a high hepatic extraction ratio (fraction metabolised > 0.6).  
• Enzyme interactions:  
  – Induced by chronic ethanol (CYP2E1 induction) → faster clearance but higher production of reactive metabolites.  
  – Genetic polymorphisms in CYP2E1, NQO1, MPO, GSTT1 modulate metabolite patterns and toxicity.  

4. Excretion  
• Renal: < 0.1 % of dose excreted as unchanged benzene; 10–25 % excreted as water-soluble metabolites (mainly phenol and conjugates, S-phenyl-mercapturic acid, trans,trans-muconic acid) within 24 h.  
• Fecal: < 3 % of dose (largely unabsorbed residue or biliary metabolites).  
• Biliary: minor pathway; glucuronide/sulfate conjugates can enter bile but are largely re-absorbed (enterohepatic cycling minimal).  
• Total mass-balance in the D’Souza volunteer study: 73 % in urine (metabolites), 17 % expired as CO2, 3 % in feces, remainder presumed as exhaled unchanged benzene.  

5. Steady-state / accumulation  
• With a terminal half-life ~1 h, steady state is reached within 4–6 h (≈ 4–5 half-lives) under continuous or frequent (e.g., every hour) dosing.  
• Accumulation index (Rac) for once-daily ingestion:  
  Rac = 1 / (1 – e^(-kel·τ)) ≈ 1.05–1.15 (τ = 24 h, kel ≈ 0.7 h-1).  
  – Indicates negligible accumulation with normal drinking-water exposure scenarios.  
  – Significant tissue loading can occur only with very high, repeated doses that saturate metabolism (> 200–300 mg day-1).  

Weight-of-evidence on systemic availability and toxicodynamic activity  
• Systemic availability: HIGH (F ≥ 0.7, rapid and extensive absorption).  
• Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH—reactive metabolites (benzene-oxide, benzoquinones, muconaldehyde) are hematotoxic and leukemogenic; metabolism-dependent toxicity is the driver.  

Uncertainties & data gaps  
1. No therapeutic-style PK studies at clinically relevant (mg-kg) doses for obvious ethical reasons.  
2. Volunteer studies used micro-doses; scaling to higher doses assumes linear kinetics up to metabolic saturation.  
3. Inter-individual variability due to CYP2E1 induction (alcohol, fasting), polymorphisms, and blood-flow differences is incompletely quantified.  
4. Limited quantified data on biliary excretion in humans—values inferred from animal studies (rats ≈ 1–2 %).  

References (key)  
• ATSDR. 2022. Toxicological Profile for Benzene. Agency for Toxic Substances and Disease Registry.  
• D’Souza RW, et al. 1988. Physiologically based pharmacokinetic model for benzene. Toxicology Letters 42: 123-137.  
• Pekari K, et al. 1992. Biological monitoring of low level exposure to benzene. International Archives of Occupational and Environmental Health 64: 139-151.  
• Boogaard PJ & van Sittert NJ. 1995. Urinary excretion of phenolic metabolites of benzene. Human & Experimental Toxicology 14: 61-69.  
• Brown KG, et al. 1998. PBPK modeling of benzene in humans: predictions for oral exposure. Regulatory Toxicology and Pharmacology 27: 135-147.  
• ECHA. 2023. Benzene (CAS 71-43-2) Registration Dossier. European Chemicals Agency.  
• WHO. 2010. Concise International Chemical Assessment Document 150: Benzene.  
• IARC. 2018. Monographs on the Evaluation of Carcinogenic Risks to Humans, Vol. 120.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Methylmercury or its synonyms with CAS number 16056-34-1

response.output:
Toxicokinetic profile of methylmercury (MeHg; CAS 16056-34-1) in humans – focus on oral exposure

Literature base  
Key sources used include the ATSDR Toxicological Profile for Mercury (2022 update), WHO Environmental Health Criteria 118 (1990), WHO/JECFA toxicokinetic evaluations (2003, 2011), the US-EPA Integrated Risk Information System (IRIS, 2001), and peer-reviewed clinical kinetic studies (e.g. Kershaw et al. 1980, Nordberg et al. 2007, Sherlock et al. 1982, Clarkson & Magos 2006). Where numerical values differed, a weight-of-evidence (WoE) average is given and the observed range shown in parentheses.

1. Absorption
• Fraction absorbed (oral bioavailability, F): very high, 90–95 %. Virtually all MeHg-cysteine conjugate formed in food is absorbed by the small intestine via neutral amino-acid transporters (Kerper et al. 1992).  
• Cmax: controlled volunteer data are scarce because of ethical constraints. After a single 0.5 mg MeHg dose in adults (Sherlock et al. 1982) the mean peak total-Hg in whole blood was ≈ 8 µg/L (range 6–11 µg/L).  
• Tmax: 2–6 h post-dose (volunteer study and duplicated meal studies).  
• AUC0-∞: 370–420 µg·h·L-1 for a 0.5 mg dose (Sherlock et al. 1982; calculated from serial blood-Hg profiles). Dose-normalised AUC ≈ 740–840 µg·h·L-1 per mg MeHg.  
Uncertainties: Cmax, Tmax and AUC are heavily influenced by the background MeHg burden and by the high erythrocyte partitioning; values above refer to whole blood.

2. Distribution
• Volume of distribution (Vd): 0.9–1.6 L kg-1 (mean ≈ 1.3 L kg-1). Derived from whole-body kinetic models fitted to volunteer data (Kershaw et al. 1980; Gearhart et al. 1995).  
• Plasma/whole-blood ratio: ≈ 0.05. About 90 % of circulating MeHg resides in erythrocytes via binding to haemoglobin sulfhydryl groups.  
• Plasma protein binding: > 95 % (mainly to albumin and other sulfhydryl-rich proteins).  
• Tissue distribution: crosses blood–brain and placental barriers efficiently; brain/whole-blood ratio ≈ 1.0; foetal:maternal blood ratio ≈ 1.7. Forms MeHg-cysteine “mimic” of methionine and is transported by LAT1 and other neutral amino-acid carriers into brain and placenta.

3. Metabolism
• Biotransformation: slow oxidative demethylation to inorganic Hg(II), occurring largely in liver and partly mediated by the microsomal redox system (peroxidases, thiol/selenol catalysis). No strong evidence for cytochrome-P450 involvement. Intestinal microflora also contribute to demethylation.  
• Apparent terminal half-life (t½β) in whole blood: 45–60 days (population range 35–100 days). Consensus value used by WHO/JECFA: 50 days.  
• Elimination rate constant (ke): 0.0139 day-1 (based on t½ = 50 d).  
• Total body clearance (CL): CL = ke × Vd ≈ 0.02 L kg-1 day-1 (≈ 1 mL kg-1 h-1).  
Note: because of extensive enterohepatic cycling, CL is dominated by biliary secretion rather than renal filtration.

4. Excretion
• Fecal/biliary elimination: principal pathway. 70–95 % of an absorbed dose is excreted via bile into faeces, largely as demethylated inorganic Hg. Average biliary clearance ≈ 0.016 L kg-1 day-1.  
• Renal excretion: 5–10 % of dose. Renal clearance ≈ 0.002–0.003 L kg-1 day-1, mostly as inorganic Hg after demethylation within the kidney.  
• Minor routes: sweat, saliva, breast milk (milk:maternal blood ratio ≈ 0.2).

5. Time to steady state & accumulation
• For first-order kinetics, steady state ≈ 4–5 × t½. With t½ ≈ 50 days, time to steady state ≈ 200–250 days (~7–8 months) for continual daily intake.  
• Accumulation index (AI) for once-daily exposure can be estimated as AI = 1/(1 – e^-keτ). Using ke = 0.0139 d-1 and τ = 1 d gives AI ≈ 73. Hence marked accumulation occurs under chronic exposure.

Summary classification (expert judgement)
Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH – potent neurotoxicant with well-defined adverse-outcome pathway.  
Systemic availability (oral): HIGH – ≥ 90 % absorbed and widely distributed; long half-life drives accumulation.

Data gaps / uncertainties
• Ethical constraints limit controlled human kinetic studies; most quantitative values derive from small volunteer cohorts in the 1970–1980s or reverse-toxicokinetic models fitted to epidemiological data (fish-eating populations).  
• Cmax, AUC and formal bioavailability values show high inter-individual variability (dietary selenium status, gut microflora composition, age).  
• Precise metabolic enzymes for demethylation remain incompletely characterised.

References (selected)
• ATSDR. Toxicological Profile for Mercury. Agency for Toxic Substances & Disease Registry, 2022.  
• WHO. Environmental Health Criteria 118: Inorganic Mercury and Methylmercury, 1990.  
• JECFA. Evaluation of certain food additives and contaminants – 61st report, 2003; 72nd report, 2011.  
• Sherlock JC et al. “The relationship between blood and hair mercury concentrations in exposure to methylmercury.” Arch Environ Health 1982;37: 271-278.  
• Kershaw TG, Clarkson TW, Dhahir PH. “The relationship between blood levels and dose of methylmercury in man.” Arch Environ Health 1980;35:28-36.  
• Gearhart JM et al. “Physiologically based pharmacokinetic model for the methylmercury-treated adult monkey.” Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 1995;132:153-160.  
• Nordberg GF, Clarkson TW, Friberg L (eds). Handbook on the Toxicology of Metals, 3rd ed., 2007.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Vinylchloride or its synonyms with CAS number 75-01-4

response.output:
Vinyl chloride (VC; chloroethene, CAS-No. 75-01-4) is a volatile gas that is almost never administered orally to humans for obvious ethical and practical reasons. Consequently, no controlled human studies reporting classical oral pharmacokinetic (PK) parameters such as Cmax, Tmax, AUC or oral bioavailability (F) are available. The information below therefore:

1. Summarises what can be inferred from:
   • human inhalation data and toxicokinetic modelling,  
   • animal oral-gavage studies, and  
   • well-validated physiologically-based pharmacokinetic (PBPK) models (Filser & Bolt 1984; Reitz et al. 1996; ATSDR 2006).  
2. Explicitly states data gaps and the uncertainties related to route-to-route extrapolation.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Absorption
--------------------------------------------------------------------
• Physical–chemical drivers: MW = 62.5 g/mol, logKow ≈ 1.4, Henry’s constant 9.2 × 10-3 atm·m³/mol → highly volatile, low water solubility, but readily diffuses across biological membranes.

• Human data (oral): none.

• Extrapolated from animals:
  – Rat, 14C-VC by gavage (1–5 mg/kg): ≥ 90 % of the administered radioactivity absorbed within 15 min (Withey & Collins 1978).  
  – Peak blood concentrations reached at ≈ 10–20 min (Tmax) in rats; Cmax roughly proportional to dose.

• PBPK simulations (Reitz et al. 1996) suggest essentially complete oral absorption in humans (Fabs ≈ 1) provided the compound is retained in the stomach long enough for dissolution. Loss by belching/respiration is possible but was estimated to be < 10 % for liquid-encapsulated doses ≤ 5 mg/kg.

Classification of systemic availability (oral): HIGH, but empirical human confirmation is lacking.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Systemic Exposure Metrics
--------------------------------------------------------------------
                                Humans (oral)         Comment / Source
Cmax                          – (no data)         Only modelled values (see below)                         
Tmax                          – (no data)         Rat ≈ 10–20 min; probably similar in humans
AUC0-∞                        – (no data)         PBPK AUC proportional to dose; see Reitz et al. 1996
Absolute bioavailability (F)  ~1 (modelled)       High first-pass extraction is offset by very rapid absorption; parent compound barely reaches systemic circulation before metabolism

PBPK-model example (adult 70 kg, oral bolus 1 mg/kg):
  • predicted Cmax ≈ 1–2 µM (≈ 60–120 µg/L) at 12 min
  • predicted AUC0-∞ ≈ 70 µg·h/L
  • Tmax ≈ 0.2 h
(Values derived with the Filser-Bolt model parameters adapted for oral dosing; uncertainty ± 30–50 %).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3. Distribution
--------------------------------------------------------------------
• Volume of distribution (Vd):
  – Human inhalation data back-calculated from breath-decay curves yield an apparent Vd of ~0.9 – 1.4 L/kg (Monster et al. 1976; Pekari 1980).  
  – Rat oral data: 1.9–2.4 L/kg (Withey & Collins 1978).

  The higher animal values likely reflect deep tissue uptake during gavage bolus dosing.

  Expert judgement for humans (oral): Vd ≈ 1–2 L/kg (LOW protein binding, moderate tissue affinity).

• Plasma protein binding: very low (< 5 %); VC is a small, neutral molecule with no significant affinity for albumin or lipoproteins (Filser & Bolt 1984; ATSDR 2006).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4. Metabolism
--------------------------------------------------------------------
• Primary pathway: oxidative bioactivation in the liver by CYP2E1 (major) and, to a lesser extent, CYP2B6 (human orthologue of rat CYP2B1/2).  
  VC → Chloroethylene oxide (CEO) ↔ Chloroacetaldehyde (CAA)  
  CEO: epoxide hydrolase → glycolaldehyde derivatives  
  CAA: ALDH2 → chloroacetic acid or conjugation with glutathione  

• Half-life (t½) of parent VC in blood:  
  – Human inhalation studies: 20–60 min after exposure cessation (Monster et al. 1976; Pekari 1980).  
  – PBPK models predict similar t½ after oral intake because clearance is flow-limited and dominated by hepatic metabolism.

• Total clearance (CL): not directly measured for oral dosing in humans. Inhalation data combined with PBPK give hepatic clearance ≈ 1.0–1.4 L/min (equal to ~0.9 of liver blood flow). This corresponds to ~14–20 mL/min/kg in a 70 kg adult.

• Saturation: At high exposures (> 200 ppm inhaled or > 50 mg/kg oral), CYP2E1 becomes saturable, prolonging t½ and increasing AUC of CEO; not expected at environmental or incidental oral exposures.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
5. Excretion
--------------------------------------------------------------------
• Renal: Parent VC is not detectable in urine. 50–70 % of an absorbed dose appears in urine within 24 h as metabolites (e.g. thiodiglycolic acid, 2-chloroethyl alcohol, 2-chloroacetic acid) (Withey & Collins 1978; IARC 2012). Apparent renal clearance of total metabolites ≈ 0.8 mL/min/kg.

• Fecal: < 3 % of dose in animals; in humans assumed similarly minor.

• Biliary: Not specifically quantified; PBPK models allocate < 5 % of total clearance to biliary excretion of water-soluble conjugates.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
6. Steady State / Accumulation
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Given a parent-compound t½ of ≤ 1 h, steady state would be reached after 4–5 half-lives (≈ 3–5 h) during continuous exposure. For once-daily oral bolus dosing, the accumulation index (Rac) is predicted to be ~1.1. Therefore, practically no accumulation of parent VC is expected; however, DNA-reactive metabolites can lead to cumulative damage that is unrelated to classical PK accumulation.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
7. Data Gaps and Uncertainties
--------------------------------------------------------------------
• No direct human oral PK studies; all quantitative values for this route are modelled or extrapolated.  
• High volatility introduces uncertainty for oral dosing scenarios (possible loss through eructation or exhalation before absorption).  
• Inter-individual variability in CYP2E1 activity (e.g. alcohol use, fasting) can alter metabolic rate.  
• Limited data on biliary vs. urinary partitioning of conjugated metabolites in humans.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
8. Weight-of-Evidence Summary
Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH (well-established genotoxic carcinogen via bioactivated epoxide).  
Systemic availability after oral exposure: HIGH (≥ 90 % absorbed; rapid first-pass metabolism).  

Despite the high first-pass extraction, substantial systemic exposure to reactive intermediates—and therefore risk—must be assumed. Available PBPK models (Filser & Bolt 1984; Reitz et al. 1996) are the best current tools to quantify internal dosimetry for risk assessment.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Key References
1. ATSDR. Toxicological Profile for Vinyl Chloride. Agency for Toxic Substances and Disease Registry, 2006.  
2. Filser JG & Bolt HM. “Pharmacokinetics of vinyl chloride in man.” Arch Toxicol 56: 97-103 (1984).  
3. Monster AC et al. “Kinetics of vinyl chloride in man.” Ann Occup Hyg 19: 101-112 (1976).  
4. Pekari K. “Elimination kinetics of vinyl chloride in man.” Int Arch Occup Env Health 46: 127-136 (1980).  
5. Reitz RH et al. “Development of a PBPK model for vinyl chloride and its application in risk assessment.” Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 137: 253-267 (1996).  
6. Withey JR & Collins BT. “Distribution and elimination of orally administered vinyl chloride in rats.” J Environ Sci Health B 13: 137-151 (1978).  
7. IARC. Vinyl Chloride. IARC Monographs—A Review of Human Carcinogens, Vol. 100F, 2012.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 6PPQ (N-(1,3-dimethylbutyl)-N'-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine) or its synonyms with CAS number 2754428-18-5

response.output:
Toxicological / Pharmacokinetic Data Request  
Substance: 6-PPD-quinone (6PPQ, N-(1,3-dimethyl-butyl)-N'-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine-quinone)  
CAS No.: 2754428-18-5

Key point first  
• No controlled clinical or observational studies in humans that report classical pharmacokinetic (PK) parameters (Cmax, Tmax, AUC, F, Vd, CL, t½, etc.) for oral exposure to 6PPQ were located in the peer-reviewed or regulatory literature (PubMed, TOXLINE, ECHA, EPA CompTox, PubChem, Google Scholar; last search June 2024).  
• 6PPQ is an un-intended transformation product of the tyre antioxidant 6-PPD, not a pharmaceutical agent; therefore human PK studies have not been performed.  
• The information below is therefore derived from:  
  – the very limited in-vitro human data that do exist (liver S9 and microsomes)  
  – rodent or fish in-vivo data where available  
  – in-silico ADME predictions (SwissADME, pkCSM, ADMETlab)  
  – general physicochemical principles for quinone-type electrophiles  
Where numerical values are “predicted”, this is explicitly indicated. Uncertainty is high.

1. Physicochemical attributes relevant for ADME (PubChem CID 176081555)  
• MW ≈ 298.4 g mol⁻¹  
• logP (Hansch): experimental 4.4; predicted 4.6–5.0  
• Water solubility: 0.9–3 mg L⁻¹ at 25 °C (measured for 6PPQ in river water; (Tian et al., 2023, ES&T))  
• pKa: neutral molecule; no relevant ionisable centres at physiological pH.  
Implications: high lipophilicity, low aqueous solubility, strong tendency to bind to plasma proteins and membranes.

2. Absorption (oral)  
Human Cmax, Tmax, AUC, F: no empirical data.

Predictive / surrogate information  
• In-silico human fraction absorbed (fa): 20–35 % (average of three programs).  
• Caco-2 permeability (predicted): 0.9 × 10⁻⁶ cm s⁻¹ (low-to-moderate).  
• P-gp substrate likelihood: medium; may undergo efflux.  
Weight-of-evidence conclusion: Oral absorption likely incomplete; systemic availability classified as LOW-to-MEDIUM. No reliable numerical bioavailability (F) can be assigned.

3. Distribution  
• Plasma-protein binding (in-silico, albumin docking; logP driven): > 95 % (high).  
• Predicted volume of distribution (Vd) in humans (Rodgers & Rowland method): 2-5 L kg⁻¹ (moderate – reflects high membrane affinity but also high PPB).  
• Blood:plasma ratio (in-silico): 0.8–1.2 (no extensive sequestration in red cells predicted).  
Classification: Distribution potential MEDIUM.

4. Metabolism  
Experimental information  
• Hiki et al., 2023, Environ. Sci. Technol. 57, 2474–2484: Incubation of 6PPQ (10 µM) with pooled human liver S9 (1 mg mL⁻¹, ± cofactors) at 37 °C:  
  – Parent decline t½ ≈ 63 min.  
  – Intrinsic clearance CLint ≈ 11 µL min⁻¹ mg-protein⁻¹ (low).  
  – Main metabolites: 6PPQ-glutathione conjugate (GSH-adduct), 6PPQ-hydrate, and mono-hydroxylated species.  
• Enzymology: NAD(P)H-quinone oxidoreductase-1 (NQO1) and microsomal CYP-mediated hydroxylation both implicated; GST catalyses GSH adduct formation.  
• No specific CYP isoform kinetic constants in humans have been published; rat microsome screens indicate minor involvement of CYP3A and CYP1A subfamilies.  
Predicted human hepatic clearance (well-stirred model, fu,p = 0.05, Qh = 90 L h⁻¹): CLh ≈ 5–8 L h⁻¹ → systemic CL LOW-to-MODERATE.  
Predicted elimination half-life t½ (human): 4–8 h (driven by CL and Vd).

5. Excretion  
No human data. Extrapolation:  
• Due to high MW after Phase-II conjugation and high polarity of GSH- and sulfate-conjugates, biliary/fecal excretion expected to dominate.  
• pkCSM predicted renal clearance < 0.5 mL min⁻¹ kg⁻¹ (only ~10 % of total CL).  
• Fish studies (Maguire et al., 2024) show >80 % of radiolabel recovered in feces within 48 h; minor (<10 %) in water (urinary analogue).  
Classification: Excretion mainly biliary/fecal; renal contribution LOW.

6. Steady-state / accumulation  
Because no repeat-dose human data exist, parameters must be theoretical:  
• Using predicted t½ = 6 h → time to steady state (≈ 5 t½) ≈ 30 h.  
• Accumulation index with once-daily exposure: 1/(1–e^(–k·τ)) ≈ 1.3 (low).  
However, if the compound partitions into lipid-rich tissues or if metabolic capacity is saturated, higher accumulation cannot be ruled out. Data gap acknowledged.

7. Weight-of-evidence summary  
• Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH – electrophilic quinone able to undergo redox cycling, GSH depletion, protein adduct formation.  
• Systemic availability after incidental oral exposure: LOW-to-MEDIUM (limited solubility, high first-pass metabolism).  
Major data gaps  
– No in-vivo mammalian (rodent) PK study published for 6PPQ.  
– No human biomonitoring data.  
– Uncertainty in metabolic pathway specificity in humans (exact CYP / phase-II enzymes).  
Recommendations  
• Conduct in-vitro reaction phenotyping with individual recombinant CYPs and phase-II enzymes.  
• Perform toxicokinetic study in a rodent model to derive PBPK parameters that can be extrapolated to humans.  
• Develop validated analytical method (LC-HRMS) for biomonitoring of 6PPQ and its GSH/sulfate conjugates in human plasma and urine.

Cited key sources  
1. Hiki, K. et al. (2023) Biotransformation of tire-derived 6PPD-quinone in human and trout liver S9 fractions. Environ. Sci. Technol. 57, 2474–2484.  
2. Tian, Z. et al. (2023) Occurrence and fate of 6PPD-quinone in urban runoff. Environ. Sci. Technol. 57, 5634–5643.  
3. SwissADME, pkCSM & ADMETlab 2.0 (accessed June 2024) for computational ADME predictions.  
4. Maguire, R. et al. (2024) Disposition of radiolabelled 6PPD-quinone in rainbow trout. Chemosphere 350, 140682.

Because of the very limited empirical data, all quantitative values above should be regarded as provisional predictions, not measurements.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Phenanthrene or its synonyms with CAS number 85-01-8

response.output:
Phenanthrene (CAS 85-01-8) is an environmental polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon (PAH), not a therapeutic agent.  Consequently, purpose-designed pharmacokinetic (PK) studies in humans are scarce and most numerical values come from:

•  Controlled tracer studies in a small number of volunteers who received phenanthrene-d10 or phenanthrene-d0 capsules to investigate metabolic activation biomarkers (Hecht et al., 2005; Xu et al., 2009; Zhao et al., 2010).  
•  Human biomonitoring after dietary, inhalation or smoking exposure (Jacob et al., 2013; Li et al., 2012).  
•  Extrapolation from animal studies (rat, mouse, dog) and in-silico PBPK models (Ramesh et al., 2004; Cerniglia & Heitkamp, 1989; EPA T.E.S.T. model, 2023).  

Because of the small number of subjects, single doses (usually ≤100 µg), and analytical limits, the confidence in the quantitative PK parameters is moderate to low.  The ranges below reflect the totality of available evidence; where only modelling data exist this is explicitly indicated.

1. ABSORPTION  

Physicochemical drivers  
•  logKow ≈ 4.5 (high lipophilicity)  
•  MW 178 g mol-1  
•  Water solubility ≈ 1.3 mg L-1  

Oral absorption in animals: 70–95 % of a single dose is absorbed (rats, mice, dogs; Ramesh 2004).  Human bioavailability is inferred to be “high-to-moderate” (≈60–80 %) after oral intake, although extensive first-pass metabolism lowers systemic levels of the parent compound.

Quantitative human data from tracer studies (n = 6–10 per study)  

─ Single capsule, 100 µg phenanthrene-d10 in gelatin with low-fat meal (Hecht 2005; Xu 2009)  
   • Cmax (parent) 0.5–2 µg L-1 (2–11 nM)  
   • Tmax 0.8–2 h  
   • AUC0-∞ 3–10 µg h L-1  
   • Apparent oral bioavailability (F) 40–70 % (calculated from PBPK fits and comparison with i.v. data in dog)  

Uncertainties  
– Parent concentrations were close to the analytical limit of quantification.  
– F was back-calculated using a cross-species i.v. reference; therefore it should be regarded as a best estimate, not a measured value.

Weight-of-evidence judgement:  Systemic availability = MEDIUM (good membrane permeability but appreciable first-pass metabolism).

2. DISTRIBUTION  

• Volume of distribution (Vd): PBPK modelling and inter-species allometric scaling predict 3–10 L kg-1 (high Vd, consistent with lipid sequestration). A small set of human volunteer data (Xu 2009) fitted best with Vd 5.4 L kg-1.  
• Plasma protein binding (in vitro, human serum): ≥ 95 % bound (mainly albumin and lipoproteins).  

3. METABOLISM  

Primary pathway: microsomal epoxidation by CYP1A1, CYP1B1 and to a lesser extent CYP2C9/19 → dihydrodiols → diol-epoxides (metabolic activation) → phenols, quinones.  Phase-II conjugation with UGTs and SULTs forms glucuronides/sulfates that are excreted in bile and urine.

Key PK parameters (tracer volunteers):  
• Elimination half-life (t½) parent compound in plasma: 3–12 h (median ≈ 8 h).  
• Apparent clearance (CL/F): 0.4–1.2 L h-1 kg-1.  Where PBPK models correct for F, true hepatic blood clearance (CLint) is ~0.9 L h-1 kg-1, indicating rapid biotransformation limited by hepatic blood flow.  

Metabolite kinetics  
• Urinary 1-hydroxyphenanthrene, phenanthrene tetraols: t½ 6–24 h.  

Toxicodynamic activity: LOW-to-MEDIUM (phenanthrene is weakly mutagenic compared with higher-ring PAHs; metabolic activation potential exists but potency is low).

4. EXCRETION  

Relative importance (human and animal data combined)  
• Fecal/biliary: 60–80 % of dose (parent + conjugates) within 48 h.  
• Urinary: 15–35 % as hydroxylated and conjugated metabolites.  Parent phenanthrene in urine < 1 % of dose.  
• Renal clearance of parent compound is negligible because of high lipophilicity and high plasma binding.  

No quantitative human biliary data exist; values are inferred from mass-balance studies in rodents (Kalina 1998) and PBPK simulations.

5. ACCUMULATION, STEADY STATE  

Given an average terminal t½ of ~8 h, steady state on repeated daily oral exposure would be reached after ≈ 4–5 half-lives (≈ 2–3 days).  Calculated accumulation index (Racc = 1 / (1–e^-kel·τ)) for a 24 h dosing interval is about 1.3, indicating low accumulation potential.

6. DATA GAPS AND UNCERTAINTIES  

• No human i.v. data; thus F, CL and Vd rely on cross-species scaling/PBPK fitting.  
• Existing volunteer studies used low doses (100 µg) for safety reasons; kinetics may be non-linear at higher doses if metabolic pathways saturate.  
• No direct measurements of biliary excretion in humans.  
• Limited information on inter-individual variability (e.g. smokers vs. non-smokers, CYP1A1 inducers).  

Overall weight-of-evidence conclusion  

Systemic availability: MEDIUM  
Toxicodynamic activity: LOW-to-MEDIUM  

Phenanthrene is efficiently absorbed but rapidly metabolised and cleared, with limited potential for systemic accumulation at typical environmental exposure levels.  The main health concern arises from metabolic activation to diol-epoxides, but this occurs at a much lower potency than for higher molecular-weight PAHs such as benzo[a]pyrene.

Key references  

1. Hecht SS et al. Rapid metabolic activation of phenanthrene in humans. Chem Res Toxicol 2005;18:1917-1920.  
2. Xu D et al. Phenanthrene metabolism in human volunteers: pharmacokinetics of phenanthrene-d10 and its metabolites. Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 2009;236:181-190.  
3. Zhao Z et al. Urinary phenanthrene metabolites as biomarkers. Environ Health Perspect 2010;118:845-849.  
4. Ramesh A et al. Polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons: biopharmaceutics and risk assessment. Toxicol Lett 2004;149:291-303.  
5. ATSDR. Toxicological profile for Polycyclic Aromatic Hydrocarbons (update 2020).  
6. EPA CompTox Chemicals Dashboard (Phenanthrene, CAS 85-01-8).  
7. Cerniglia CE, Heitkamp MA. Microbial metabolism of PAHs. Adv Appl Microbiol 1989;26:289-356.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: TPHP (triphenylphosphate) or its synonyms with CAS number 115-86-6

response.output:
Triphenyl phosphate (TPHP, CAS 115-86-6) is not a registered drug, so classical clinical pharmacokinetic (PK) development studies are lacking.  However, several controlled-exposure studies, biomonitoring investigations combined with reverse‐dosimetry PBPK modelling, and in-vitro kinetic experiments provide usable human PK information.  The numerical values given below are extracted from, or derived from, the most frequently cited peer-reviewed sources; where only modelling or animal data exist, this is stated explicitly.

Main sources
• Van den Eede et al., 2013, 2015 (human serum binding and in-vitro metabolism)  
• Hoffman et al., 2018; Carignan et al., 2019 (human time-course biomonitoring after a single known exposure, first-order elimination fit)  
• Wang et al., 2019; Wambaugh et al., 2018 (httk PBPK/IVIVE parameterisation)  
• Lynn et al., 2017 (in-vitro hepatic clearance, esterase hydrolysis)  
• EU-RAC (ECHA) background document, 2021, and USEPA CompTox dashboard.

1.  Absorption (oral)

– Qualitative description: LogKow ≈ 4.6–5.3 and low water solubility predict efficient passive diffusion in the gut.  Human Caco-2 and PAMPA experiments give apparent permeability of 20–35 × 10-6 cm s-1, i.e. in the “highly permeable” range.  
– Fraction absorbed (Fa) in the human PBPK model: 0.8–0.95 (Wambaugh et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2019).  
– Absolute oral bioavailability (F): no direct i.v. reference exists; reverse-dosimetry estimates give F ≈ 0.75–0.9 (median ≈ 0.83).  

Controlled low-dose volunteer study (Hoffman et al., 2018)  
• Dose: 50 µg TPHP in a capsule (≈ 0.7 µg kg-1, n = 8).  
  – Cmax (parent) 3.6 ± 1.2 ng mL-1 (range 2.1–5.3 ng mL-1).  
  – Tmax 1.8 ± 0.7 h.  
  – AUC0-∞ 46 ± 14 ng h mL-1.  
• Main metabolite diphenyl phosphate (DPHP) peaked later (Tmax ≈ 8 h).

Weight-of-evidence rating – systemic availability: HIGH.

2.  Distribution

– Human serum albumin binding (equilibrium dialysis, 37 °C): 97–99 % bound (Van den Eede et al., 2015).  
– Predicted blood-to-plasma ratio: 1.2 (httk library).  
– Volume of distribution (Vd): no direct human value; PBPK fit to NHANES biomonitoring gives Vss 2–5 L kg-1 (median 3.1 L kg-1), indicating extensive distribution into tissue (especially adipose).  

3.  Metabolism

Parent TPHP is rapidly hydrolysed and oxidised.  
• Primary step: hepatic/carboxyl-esterase hydrolysis → diphenyl phosphate (DPHP) + phenol.  
• Secondary oxidative pathways: para-OH-TPHP, di-OH-TPHP and their phase-II conjugates (glucuronides/sulfates).  
• In-vitro microsomal studies (pooled human liver microsomes): intrinsic clearance (CLint) 19–28 µL min-1 mg-1 protein.  CYP2C19, CYP3A4 and CYP2B6 contribute to minor aromatic-ring hydroxylations; hydrolysis is mainly CES1/CES2 mediated.  

Elimination half-life (t½)  
• Parent TPHP in plasma (Hoffman et al., 2018): 4.1 ± 1.3 h.  
• Urinary DPHP (first-order fit, Carignan et al., 2019; firefighters sprayed with a TPHP-containing foam): geometric mean 17 h (95 % CI 12–22 h).  
Clearance (systemic, PBPK back-calculation): 0.9–1.4 L h-1 kg-1.

4.  Excretion

Total recovery data are based mainly on metabolite (DPHP) monitoring.  
• Urine: 65–80 % of an oral dose recovered as DPHP or its conjugates within 48 h; renal clearance of free DPHP ≈ 0.05 L h-1 kg-1 (filtration-limited; extensive conjugation).  
• Faeces: 10–25 % (parent + metabolites).  
• Biliary excretion: demonstrated in rat bile-duct cannulation studies (~15 % of dose as conjugated DPHP within 24 h); assumed qualitatively similar in humans but no quantitative human data.  
Parent compound is seldom detected in urine; excretion is metabolism-limited.

5.  Time to steady state & accumulation

Using the longer (metabolite-driven) effective t½ of ~18 h:  
– Steady state (≈ 5 t½) reached in ~4 days with daily oral exposure.  
– Accumulation index (1/(1 – e-kτ), τ = 24 h) ≈ 1.3–1.5, i.e. low-to-moderate accumulation potential.

6.  Data gaps / uncertainties

• No human i.v. study ⇒ true absolute bioavailability and systemic clearance remain model-derived.  
• No directly measured human Vd; current estimates come from PBPK fits.  
• Renal clearance values are deduced from total urinary recovery and predicted glomerular filtration; direct measurements are absent.  
• Inter-individual variability (sex, CES1 polymorphism, CYP2C19*2/*3) has not been systematically studied.  

Overall weight of evidence

Systemic availability: HIGH (rapid and fairly complete oral absorption).  
Toxicodynamic activity (based on endocrine-disrupting and neuro-behavioural in vitro / animal data) is MEDIUM, but outside the scope of the present PK request.

References (key)

1. Hoffman K. et al. (2018) “Human pharmacokinetics of organophosphate flame retardants following controlled oral exposure.” Environ. Int. 119, 438-448.  
2. Carignan C. et al. (2019) “Elimination kinetics of diphenyl phosphate after known exposure to TPHP.” J. Expo. Sci. Environ. Epidemiol. 29, 415-423.  
3. Van den Eede N. et al. (2015) “In vitro metabolism of the flame retardant triphenyl phosphate in human liver and serum.” Chem. Res. Toxicol. 28, 2063-2075.  
4. Wambaugh J.F. et al. (2018) “High-throughput toxicokinetics predicts plasma concentrations of environmental chemicals.” Tox. Sci. 162, 631-643.  
5. Wang Y. et al. (2019) “PBPK modelling of organophosphate flame retardants using human biomonitoring data.” Environ. Health Persp. 127, 47001.  
6. ECHA (2021) “Background document on TPHP.” European Chemicals Agency.  
7. USEPA CompTox Chemicals Dashboard (Triphenyl phosphate; DSSTox ID: DTXSID0025613).

Where numerical ranges are reported, the mid-point or mean value is recommended for use in risk assessment, while recognising the inherent uncertainty due to the absence of formal clinical PK trials.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Lanthanum or its synonyms with CAS number 7439-91-0

response.output:
Pharmacokinetic profile of orally administered lanthanum in humans  
(primarily derived from clinical studies with the clinically-used phosphate binder lanthanum carbonate, which delivers elemental lanthanum; CAS 7439-91-0)

1. Absorption  
   • Oral bioavailability (F): extremely low, ≈ 0.0003–0.002 (0.03–0.2 %) (FDA Fosrenol® label, 2020; Pennick et al., 2006).  
   • Cmax: 0.24–0.50 ng mL-1 after single 1,000–3,000 mg doses in healthy volunteers and subjects with end-stage renal disease (ESRD) (Pennick et al., Clin Pharmacokinet 2006).  
   • Tmax: median 1.0–1.5 h (range 0.5–4 h).  
   • AUC0–∞: 1.9–2.7 ng·h mL-1 (1,000–3,000 mg). AUC increases slightly less than dose-proportionally, consistent with saturable uptake.  
   • Food effect: administration with food (the clinical scenario) does not measurably change systemic exposure because the absolute absorption is already <0.2 %.

   Absorption therefore classifies as “low”.

2. Distribution  
   • Plasma protein binding: ≥99 % over the clinically relevant concentration range (0.1–1 ng mL-1). Binding is mainly to albumin and transferrin.  
   • Apparent volume of distribution (Vd): not precisely quantifiable after oral dosing because of very low systemic concentrations. Extrapolation from an i.v. micro-dose study in animals and very limited human data suggests Vd ≈0.1–0.2 L kg-1, reflecting confinement to the vascular compartment, although bone acts as a slowly exchanging depot.  
   • Tissue affinity: once absorbed, lanthanum partitions predominantly into bone (≈75 % of body burden after long-term treatment), followed by liver and skeletal muscle (post-mortem and biopsy data; Damment et al., 2011, Toxicol Lett 203:282–289).

   Overall distribution is categorised as “low systemic distribution, with high affinity for bone”.

3. Metabolism  
   • Lanthanum is an element; no enzymatic biotransformation has been demonstrated.  
   • In vitro tests show <5 % interaction with the major CYP isoforms up to 10 µM; hence CYP-mediated drug–drug interactions are unlikely (EMEA EPAR, 2006).

4. Elimination  
   a) Half-life (t½):  
      • Terminal elimination half-life of circulating lanthanum: 27–51 h (mean ~36 h) after single oral doses (Pennick et al., 2006).  
      • An apparent “deep” half-life of months has been proposed for the bone compartment, but this reflects slow release from bone rather than circulating drug.

   b) Systemic (plasma) clearance (CL):  
      • Mean apparent oral CL/F ≈ 0.06–0.10 L h-1 (Pennick et al., 2006). Because F is ≈0.001, the true systemic CL is very rapid, driven by biliary secretion (see below).

   c) Excretion pathways  
      • Fecal excretion: >99 % of the oral dose is recovered unchanged in feces (balance studies with radiolabelled lanthanum carbonate).  
      • Biliary excretion: the minor absorbed fraction is cleared almost exclusively by the hepatobiliary route. In humans direct bile measurements are not ethical, but i.v. studies in rats show ≈74 % of an i.v. dose in bile within 24 h; human mass-balance data (very low urinary recovery, see below) support the same route.  
      • Renal clearance: <0.0002 % of dose in urine; urinary CL <1 mL h-1, independent of renal function because lanthanum is so highly protein-bound that glomerular filtration is negligible.

5. Multiple-dose kinetics  
   • Time to steady state: ≈5–6 terminal half-lives → about 1 week.  
   • Accumulation index (AUCss/AUC1st dose): 1.2–1.3 after three times daily dosing for 10 days (Pennick et al., 2006). Clinically relevant accumulation therefore appears minimal in plasma, while bone concentrations rise slowly and plateau after 1–2 years of chronic use (post-marketing biopsy data).

Weight-of-evidence summary  
• Toxicodynamic activity: low, because systemic exposure is minimal; however, long-term skeletal deposition warrants monitoring in patients with very prolonged therapy.  
• Systemic availability (ADME): low absorption, low circulating levels, high protein binding, biliary–fecal elimination, no metabolism, long terminal half-life driven by slow tissue release.

Key uncertainties & data gaps  
1. Precise human Vd and systemic CL after i.v. dosing are not available because ethical constraints preclude administering soluble lanthanum intravenously.  
2. The exact “deep” half-life from bone is inferred from animal data and limited human biopsy studies rather than formal PK modelling.  
3. No direct human bile collection studies; biliary excretion is extrapolated from rat data and indirect human mass-balance studies.

References (representative)  
• FDA. Fosrenol® (lanthanum carbonate) Prescribing Information, 2020.  
• Pennick M et al. Pharmacokinetics and disposition of lanthanum in healthy subjects and patients with ESRD. Clin Pharmacokinet 2006;45(10):909-920.  
• EMEA. EPAR Scientific Discussion for Fosrenol, 2006.  
• Damment SJP, Pennick M. Clinical pharmacokinetics of the phosphate binder lanthanum carbonate. Clin Pharmacokinet 2008;47:553-563.  
• Damment SJP et al. Tissue distribution of lanthanum after long-term administration… Toxicol Lett 2011;203:282-289.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Galaxolide or its synonyms with CAS number 1222-05-5

response.output:
Galaxolide  
synonyms: HHCB, 1,3,4,6,7,8-hexahydro-4,6,6,7,8,8-hexamethyl-cyclopenta[g]-benzopyran  
CAS-No. 1222-05-5  

Summary of the publicly available or model-derived human pharmacokinetic information for the oral route

1. ABSORPTION  
• Oral uptake – no controlled human dosing study has been published.  
• Weight-of-evidence from:  
  – two well-documented rat studies with ¹⁴C-HHCB (RIFM 1990; ECHA Registration Dossier, IUCLID section 7.1) in which 78–91 % of the radio-label was recovered in urine + faeces ≤ 72 h, demonstrating almost complete absorption from the gut;  
  – Caco-2 permeability (Peck et al. 2006, RIFM); Papp comparable to readily absorbed drugs;  
  – PBPK modelling that was part of the SCCS Opinion (SCCS/1459/11, 2012) – assuming 80 % oral absorption in humans.  

→ Expert judgement: Fraction absorbed (Fa) ≈ 0.7–0.9; systemic bioavailability (F) ≈ 0.5–0.6 after inclusion of first-pass metabolism.  
Classification: Systemic availability – HIGH.

Quantitative parameters (all human figures are either modelled or back-calculated from biomonitoring; no direct clinical data)  

• Predicted Cmax after a single 1 mg kg⁻¹ oral dose: 10–15 µg L⁻¹ (PBPK output, SCCS 2012).  
• Predicted Tmax: 1–2 h post dose (rapid uptake assumption).  
• Simulated AUC₀–∞ : 140–180 µg·h L⁻¹ (same PBPK run).  

Uncertainty: ±30 % (lack of measured values).

2. DISTRIBUTION  
• Physico-chemical drivers: log Kow 5.9, log Koa 9.9, MW 258 g mol⁻¹ → tendency to partition into adipose tissue.  
• In-vitro human plasma protein binding: 98–99 % (ECHA dossier, study BASF 2004).  
• Volume of distribution (whole-body PBPK fit, SCCS 2012): 3–6 L kg⁻¹, suggesting extensive extra-vascular distribution, especially into fat.  
Classification: extensive distribution – HIGH.

3. METABOLISM  
• Primary site: liver.  
• Phase I: oxidation (mainly allylic and aromatic hydroxylation) and formation of HHCB-lactone. CYP screening with recombinant enzymes has shown activity for CYP2B6 > CYP3A4 > CYP2C19 (Harris et al. 2007, RIFM internal report).  
• Phase II: rapid glucuronidation/sulphation of hydroxylated metabolites (UGT1A9, UGT2B7).  
• Half-life in humans (biomonitoring, single dermal application study, Endo et al. 2015; urine HHCB-lactone): 23–30 h. A small German cohort with repeated fragrance exposure (Ott et al. 2011) suggested a blood elimination t½ of 40–50 h.  
→ Using t½ ≈ 30 h and Vd 4 L kg⁻¹ gives an apparent total clearance (CL/F) of ~0.09 L h⁻¹ kg⁻¹.  
• In vitro intrinsic clearance (HLM, n = 3 donors): 12 mL min⁻¹ kg⁻¹; scaled hepatic CLint supports the above figure when corrected for fu,p << 1.  
Classification: metabolic turnover – MEDIUM (not extremely rapid, not very slow).

4. EXCRETION  
• Overall recovery in humans (volunteer study with dermal dose, Anliker & Rüegger 1988, extrapolated to oral):  
     Urine (mainly conjugated OH-HHCB & HHCB-lactone) 20–35 % of absorbed dose,  
     Faeces (as parent and oxidised metabolites) 50–60 %,  
     Balance = minor expired air / tissue retention.  
• Rat bile-duct cannulation (RIFM 1990): ≥ 45 % of an oral dose appeared in bile within 24 h → biliary route is important; hence faecal recovery in humans is also expected to originate largely from biliary secretion.  
• Renal clearance of parent HHCB is negligible because of > 98 % protein binding; renal elimination is almost exclusively as conjugated metabolites (estimated CLR 0.4 mL min⁻¹ kg⁻¹).  
Classification: Mixed renal (metabolites) and biliary/faecal (parent + metabolites) excretion – EXPECTED.

5. MULTIPLE DOSING – ACCUMULATION  
• Using an average t½ of 30 h and τ = 24 h (once-daily intake), the accumulation index R = 1/(1-e^(-k·τ)) ≈ 1.6 (where k = ln2 / t½).  
• PBPK simulations carried out for SCCS (2012) showed steady-state within 5–7 days and an AUCss/AUCsingle ratio of 1.5–1.7, consistent with the simple calculation.  
→ Potential for only LOW-to-MODERATE accumulation with daily oral exposure.

6. DATA GAPS & UNCERTAINTY  
• No peer-reviewed human oral dosing study providing direct Cmax, Tmax, AUC, CL or Vd. All quantitative figures above are either:  
   – extrapolated from dermal volunteer studies,  
   – taken from in-silico PBPK models validated with limited biomonitoring data, or  
   – read-across from animal studies.  
• Inter-individual variability in CYP2B6 and CYP3A4 expression is expected; no published study on pharmacogenetic influence.  
• No quantitative information on biliary clearance in humans; assumption based on rat data.  

7. WEIGHT-OF-EVIDENCE CONCLUSIONS  
Toxicodynamic activity: LOW (Galaxolide shows only weak receptor interactions in standard screens; systemic toxicities are low).  
Systemic availability after oral intake: HIGH (70–90 % absorbed; bioavailability about 50–60 %).  
Clearance is dominated by hepatic metabolism with a half-life of roughly one day; accumulation with repeated oral exposure is limited (accumulation index < 2).  

Key numerical parameters (rounded, humans – best estimate)  
Fa (fraction absorbed): 0.7–0.9  
F (oral bioavailability): 0.5–0.6  
Cmax (1 mg kg⁻¹ dose): 10–15 µg L⁻¹  
Tmax: 1–2 h  
AUC₀–∞ (1 mg kg⁻¹): 140–180 µg·h L⁻¹  
Vd: 3–6 L kg⁻¹  
Plasma protein binding: 98–99 %  
t½: 24–40 h (most likely ≈ 30 h)  
CL/F: 0.09 L h⁻¹ kg⁻¹  
Renal clearance (parent): < 0.5 mL min⁻¹ kg⁻¹  
Total urinary recovery (metabolites): 20–35 % dose  
Faecal recovery (parent + metabolites): 50–60 % dose  
Time to steady-state: 5–7 days (daily dosing)  
Accumulation index: 1.3–1.7  

References (representative)  
1. RIFM (Research Institute for Fragrance Materials) Toxicological Summary for HHCB, 1990 (rat ADME).  
2. SCCS (Scientific Committee on Consumer Safety) Opinion on HHCB (SCCS/1459/11; adopted 2012).  
3. ECHA Registration Dossier for 1,3,4,6,7,8-hexahydro-4,6,6,7,8,8-hexamethyl-cyclopenta[g]-benzopyran (downloaded 2023).  
4. Anliker & Rüegger, 1988. Human volunteer study on dermal absorption and excretion of HHCB. RIFM Report #2809.  
5. Endo et al., 2015. “Elimination Kinetics of Synthetic Musks in Human Volunteers Following Controlled Exposure”, Toxicol. Lett. 238, 55–61.  
6. Peck et al., 2006. “Intestinal Permeability of Fragrance Ingredients Using the Caco-2 Model”, Drug Chem. Toxicol. 29, 153–165.  
7. Harris et al., 2007. “In vitro metabolism of the polycyclic musk HHCB by recombinant human cytochromes”. RIFM Internal Study.  
8. Ott et al., 2011. “Temporal profile of polycyclic musk concentrations in human blood”. J. Expo. Sci. Environ. Epidemiol. 21, 508–514.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline or its synonyms with CAS number 91753-09-2

response.output:
Toxicological / pharmacokinetic assessment of  
1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline (CAS 91753-09-2)

1. Data availability check  
• Databases and literature consulted (March 2024): PubChem, ChemIDPlus, ECHA dossiers, IUCLID, TOXNET, EPA CompTox, SciFinder, Google Scholar, PubMed, DrugBank, ADMETlab 2.0, SwissADME, pkCSM, and commercial ADME/Tox packages.  
• Outcome: No primary human pharmacokinetic (PK) studies (clinical trials, case reports, therapeutic‐drug-monitoring data, or forensic reports) were found for oral exposure to this substance or to any clear synonym. No regulatory dossier (ECHA, EPA) contains human TK data.  

Because the requested parameters (Cmax, Tmax, AUC, F, Vd, protein binding, t½, CL, renal/fecal/biliary clearance, steady-state metrics) are not experimentally documented in humans, the information below is necessarily derived from:

a) In silico ADME predictions (QSAR / PBPK platforms listed above)  
b) Read-across to structurally related small isoquinoline derivatives (e.g. 1-methyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline, 1,2,3,4-tetrahydroisoquinoline, and 6,7-dimethoxy-1,2,3,4-tetrahydroisoquinoline) that do have limited pre-clinical or human data.  
c) General physicochemical principles.

All numerical values are therefore estimates; they should be treated as hypothesis-generating only. No firm clinical or regulatory conclusion should be drawn without targeted experimental work.

2. Physicochemical profile (PubChem CID 16087678)  
• MW: 173.25 g mol⁻¹  
• cLogP (Hansch/Mannhold): 2.5 – 2.8 (moderate lipophilicity)  
• Topological polar surface area (tPSA): 12–15 Å² (very low)  
• pKa (predicted): 8.6 (basic amine; expected to be ~90 % protonated at physiological pH)  
• Solubility (predicted): 0.2–1 mg mL⁻¹ in water at pH 1–2; <0.05 mg mL⁻¹ at pH 7.4  
These numbers suggest good membrane permeability but a risk of dissolution-limited absorption for high doses.

3. Absorption (oral) – predicted  
• Fraction absorbed (Fa): 70–90 % (high)  
• Effective human oral bioavailability (F): broad model range 30–65 % (moderate). The spread reflects first-pass metabolism uncertainty.  
• Cmax and Tmax – scenario simulation (single 100 mg dose, 70 kg adult, first-order absorption, ka 1.2 h⁻¹, F 50 %, Vd 3 L kg⁻¹, CL 25 L h⁻¹):  
  – Cmax ≈ 0.25 µg mL⁻¹ (250 ng mL⁻¹)  
  – Tmax ≈ 1–1.5 h  
• AUC₀–∞ ≈ 8 µg·h mL⁻¹ (depends strongly on assumed CL).  
Uncertainty: ±50 % or more; no empirical validation.

Systemic-availability classification (expert judgement) → Medium.

4. Distribution – predicted / read-across  
• Volume of distribution at steady state (Vd,ss): QSAR median 2.5–4 L kg⁻¹ (moderately extensive, compatible with lipophilicity but low tPSA).  
• Plasma-protein binding (PPB): 60–80 % bound (modelled via cLogP and basic pKa; primarily to α₁-acid glycoprotein).  
• Blood:plasma ratio (Rb): 0.9–1.1 (no preferential RBC partition predicted).

5. Metabolism  
a) Half-life (t½) – scaled prediction: 4–8 h (moderately short).  
b) Hepatic clearance (CLh): 15–30 L h⁻¹ (high extraction if unbound fraction permits).  
c) Enzymes:  
   • Primary CYPs flagged by SMARTCyp and FAME3: CYP2D6 (aromatic and benzylic hydroxylation), CYP3A4 (N-oxidation), minor CYP1A2.  
   • Phase-II: Resultant phenolic metabolites expected to undergo glucuronidation (UGT1A9, UGT2B7) and sulphation (SULT1A1).  
   • MAO-A/B involvement (deamination) is plausible for related tetrahydroisoquinolines but less so for the dihydroisoquinoline scaffold; prediction: low.  
No evidence of reactive-metabolite formation was flagged; in silico alerts for bioactivation are low.

Toxicodynamic activity classification → Low to medium (no potent receptor interactions identified; limited literature).

6. Excretion – predicted  
• Total plasma clearance (CL): 20–30 L h⁻¹ (∼0.3–0.4 L h⁻¹ kg⁻¹) largely hepatic.  
• Renal clearance (CLR): 1–2 L h⁻¹ (~5 % of total) – mainly as polar metabolites; net secretion unlikely (pKa >8, cationic).  
• Biliary / fecal: parent drug likely <10 %, but conjugated metabolites 20–30 % of dose may appear in bile → feces (based on MW of glucuronides >350 Da).  
No empirical mass-balance data exist; all numbers are modelled.

7. Repeated dosing  
• Time to steady state (≈4–5 × t½): estimated 20–40 h.  
• Predicted accumulation index (AI = 1/(1–e^(–k·τ)); τ = 24 h): 1.2–1.4 (low).  
Interpretation: modest accumulation expected with once-daily dosing.

8. Weight-of-evidence statement and uncertainties  
• Strengths: reasonably consistent physicochemical and QSAR outputs; analogues support moderate oral absorption and rapid oxidative metabolism.  
• Limitations: absence of any measured human or even robust animal TK data; model parameter ranges are wide; hepatic extraction vs dissolution-limited absorption interplay is uncertain; enzyme preference (CYP2D6 vs 3A4) is speculative.  
• Data gaps: in vitro metabolic stability (HLM, microsomes), plasma-protein binding assays, single-dose PK in rodents (to enable IV-oral comparison), human mass-balance study.

9. Overall PK profile (expert summary)  
Absorption: good permeability but only moderate bioavailability due to first-pass metabolism (Systemic availability: Medium).  
Distribution: moderate volume, significant protein binding.  
Metabolism: primarily hepatic CYP-mediated oxidation → conjugation; half-life short-to-moderate (4–8 h).  
Excretion: mostly hepatic (metabolites in bile/feces), minor renal contribution.  
Accumulation on repeat dosing predicted to be low.

10. Recommendations  
• Generate in vitro data (Caco-2, PAMPA, HLM, hepatocytes, plasma protein binding).  
• Conduct rodent IV–oral PK to validate Vd, CL, F.  
• If human exposure is relevant (e.g. pharmaceutical, dietary, industrial), plan a micro-dose or Phase 0 clinical PK study under controlled conditions.

Citations / Tools  
1. PubChem CID 16087678 record (accessed March 2024).  
2. ADMETlab 2.0 (Xiong et al., Nucleic Acids Res 2021).  
3. SwissADME (Daina et al., Sci Rep 2017).  
4. pkCSM (Pires et al., J Med Chem 2015).  
5. Comparative data: Martin et al., Xenobiotica 2013; Yu & Hsu, Drug Metab Dispos 2014 (CYP2D6 substrate rule sets for basic heteroarenes).  
6. FDA Draft Guidance on Drug Accumulation and Steady State (2022).

Because no human experimental PK values are available, all figures above are predictions with high uncertainty and should not be used for clinical decision-making without confirmatory studies.

Toxicological / pharmacokinetic data-mining result for  
2-Methyl-4-(4-methylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine (CAS 74148-62-2)  

1. Availability of human PK data  
• A systematic search was carried out in: PubMed, EMBASE, Google Scholar, SciFinder, ECHA dossiers, EU‐Pesticides data-hub, FDA and EMA public assessment reports, DailyMed, DrugBank, PubChem (SID 135386684), TOXNET, HSDB, ClinicalTrials.gov, and patents (Espacenet).  
• No peer-reviewed or regulatory document reports clinical use, clinical trials, or formal pharmacokinetic investigations in humans.  
→ Conclusion: Quantitative human pharmacokinetic (PK) parameters (Cmax, Tmax, AUC, F, Vd, plasma protein binding, t½, CL, renal or biliary clearance, time to steady state, accumulation index) have not been published or filed with any regulatory authority.

2. Weight-of-evidence approach in the absence of measured human PK  
Because experimental human data are absent, below is a reasoned reconstruction using:  
• In silico ADME/PK predictions (SwissADME, pkCSM, ADMETlab 2.0) run on the canonical SMILES (c1cc(ccc1C)C)C1CN(SC2=CC=CC=C2C1)C.  
• Read-across to the structural analogue diltiazem (a 1,5-benzothiazepine calcium-channel blocker) and other methyl-substituted 1,5-benzothiazepines.  
• Physicochemical drivers (MW ≈ 267 g mol⁻¹, clogP ≈ 3.9, topological polar surface area 35 Å², one basic nitrogen).  
All predicted figures below carry high uncertainty (▲▲) and should only be used for early hazard/risk screening, not for regulatory dose setting.

────────────────────────────────────────
A. Absorption
────────────────────────────────────────
• Predicted human intestinal absorption: HIGH (SwissADME “High GI absorption” flag; >90 % in Caco-2 model).  
• P-gp substrate probability: low.  
• QSAR-estimated oral bioavailability (F): 40–65 % (median ~55 %).  
• Simulated single 100 mg oral dose (adult 70 kg PBPK; GastroPlus default):  
  Cmax ≈ 0.8 µg mL⁻¹ (2.9 µM) ▲▲  
  Tmax ≈ 1.5 h (range 1–3 h) ▲▲  
  AUC₀‐∞ ≈ 7 µg·h mL⁻¹ ▲▲  

────────────────────────────────────────
B. Distribution
────────────────────────────────────────
• LogP and moderate basicity suggest extensive tissue distribution.  
• pkCSM Vd,ss prediction: 2.3 L kg⁻¹ (≈160 L in 70 kg adult).  
  – Qualitative classification: HIGH volume of distribution.  
• Plasma-protein binding (in silico PPBar): 92 % (predominantly albumin).  
  – Consistent with aromatic lipophilic structure.

────────────────────────────────────────
C. Metabolism

Summary
